steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

# Regression QA - RC 2.14: transaction-categorization findings

**Test round:** RC 2.14 pre-release categorization pass
**Prepared by:** QA (enrichment)
**Verdict:** Not signed off - the findings below must clear before this build ships

## Summary

The RC 2.14 pass replays our labelled statement-fixture corpus through the
enrichment stage and diffs every derived field against the accepted baseline.
The overwhelming majority of fixtures reproduce their baseline labels exactly.
A thin residue of rows, though, now derive a category or a counterparty name
that disagrees with the accepted answer. Each regressed row is stable - the same
fixture yields the identical wrong field every time it is replayed - and each one
hinges on a narrow, individually rare pattern in the underlying reference text
rather than on the size of the amount or on which customer it belongs to.
Ordinary purchases, routine transfers and everyday deposits all reproduce
baseline, which is why the aggregate diff read clean until these edge fixtures
were isolated.

The findings are recorded in the order they surfaced. QA has not traced any of
them to a root cause; that is left to engineering. Acceptance for this build is
simply that every listed finding derives its baseline field again and that no
fixture already matching baseline moves.

## Findings

**F-1.** A cheque-type debit whose statement note carries nothing but the
instrument's own number - a short, bare run of digits with no other text sitting
beside it - is landing in the generic transfer bucket. Cheque debits whose notes
are shaped even slightly differently keep their correct cheque label, so the miss
is confined to this one bare-number note form.

**F-2.** On a cleared cheque, the counterparty column is coming back with an
internal clearing tag in place of the account-holder's name. The affected rows
all share one raw-narration layout, copied here from a fixture:

> `CHQ PAID-MICR CLG-RAVI KUMAR`

For this layout the field is returning a fragment of the reference instead of the
name that should be lifted from it.

**F-3.** A slice of inbound electronic-transfer credits - the plain
national-transfer kind - are arriving with the counterparty-name column left
completely blank. The category on these rows is right; it is only the name that
goes missing. Other electronic-transfer credits, whose references are laid out
differently, still resolve their names normally, so this is not a blanket failure
of name extraction.

**F-4.** An incoming person-to-person instant-payment credit is showing a bare
reference number where the sender's name belongs. The value that surfaces is a
fragment of the very reference string the name should have been read from;
neighbouring instant-payment layouts still resolve their names correctly.

**F-5.** A small nominal deposit that a bank posts purely to confirm an account
is reachable - the token "is this account live" credit rather than a real
payment - is being filed as an ordinary transfer instead of being recognised as
an account-verification entry. Ordinary deposits are untouched; only these token
confirmation credits regressed.

## Exit criteria

The enrichment path is fully deterministic and runs offline, so each finding
above is reproducible on demand from the fixture that triggered it. Restore the
correct category and counterparty on the listed forms without disturbing any
fixture that already agrees with baseline. A change that rescues these rows but
shifts the label or the name of ordinary, currently-correct traffic does not meet
acceptance: the bar is that the specific regressions listed here clear and
everything else stays exactly where the baseline has it.

    EXPENSE_LIST = ["VEHICLE_EXPENSES","CHILD_EXPENSES","ENTERTAINMENT","HEALTHCARE","INVESTMENT","SHOPPING","GENERAL_EXPENSES","RESTAURANTS_DINING","LOAN_REPAY
MENT","TRAVEL","BILL_PAYMENT","COMMUTE","HOME_IMPROVEMENT","ATM_WITHDRAWAL","AUTO_PAYMENT","CREDIT_CARD_PAYMENT"]
    INCOME_LIST = ["SALARY","INVESTMENT_INCOME","RENT_INCOME","REVERSAL","AUTO_CREDIT","OTHER_INCOME","REIMBURSEMENT"]
    TRANSFER_LIST = ["TRANSFER","LOAN"]

    conditions_choices = [
        [transactions.pfmCategoryOne.isin(EXPENSE_LIST), "EXPENSE"],
        [transactions.pfmCategoryOne.isin(INCOME_LIST),"INCOME"],
        [transactions.pfmCategoryOne.isin(TRANSFER_LIST), "TRANSFER"]
    ]

    df = pd.DataFrame(data=conditions_choices, columns=['conditions', 'choices'])
    transactions['pfmCategoryType'] = np.select(df['conditions'], df['choices'], default="UNCATEGORISED")

    return (transactions)root@f1745c17-6452-41e1-84ed-32f69853bde7:/app#

import time
from flask import Flask, g
import logging
import warnings
import pandas as pd

app = Flask(__name__)

# ignore all caught warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

app.logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

# to capture the start time of request
@app.before_request
def before_request():
    g.start = time.time()

# to calculate total time taken for request-respone
@app.after_request
def after_request(response):
    diff = time.time() - g.start
    app.logger.info(f"Total time taken : {diff}s")
    return response

pd.set_option('display.max_rows', None)

import categorizationapp.Controller

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=9898, debug=True)root@f1745c17-6452-41e1-84ed-32f69853bde7:/app/categorizationapp#

import json
from categorizationapp import app

client = app.test_client()

def run(desc, ttype, amount, remark="", bank="HDFC", acc="SAVING"):
    payload = {
        "bank": bank,
        "acc_type": acc,
        "accountUID": "u1",
        "accountHolder": "",
        "transactions": [
            {"description": desc, "type": ttype, "amount": amount,
             "balanceAfterTransaction": 1000, "remark": remark}
        ]
    }
    r = client.post("/categorizeTransaction", json=payload)
    d = r.get_json()["response"][0]
    print(f"desc={desc!r}")
    print(f"   cat={d.get('transactionCategory')!r} party={d.get('partyName')!r}")

# F-2
run("CHQ PAID-MICR CLG-RAVI KUMAR", "DEBIT", 5000)
PYEOF
cd /app/categorizationapp && python3 /app/repro.py

import json
from categorizationapp import app
client = app.test_client()
def run(label, desc, ttype, amount, remark="", bank="CANARA", acc="CURRENT"):
    payload = {"bank": bank,"acc_type": acc,"accountUID": "u1","accountHolder": "",
        "transactions": [{"description": desc, "type": ttype, "amount": amount,
             "balanceAfterTransaction": 1000, "remark": remark}]}
    r = client.post("/categorizeTransaction", json=payload)
    j = r.get_json()
    if 'response' not in j: print(f"[{label}] ERR {j}"); return
    d = j["response"][0]
    print(f"[{label}] {desc!r} -> cat={d.get('transactionCategory')!r} party={d.get('partyName')!r}")

# F-1 cheque bare number debit
run("F1a", "123456", "DEBIT", 5000)
run("F1b", "000123", "DEBIT", 5000)
# F-2
run("F2", "CHQ PAID-MICR CLG-RAVI KUMAR", "DEBIT", 5000)
# F-3 NEFT credit
run("F3a", "NEFT-SBIN123456-RAVI KUMAR", "CREDIT", 5000)
run("F3b", "NEFT CR-SBIN0001234-RAVI KUMAR-SB", "CREDIT", 5000)
# F-4 IMPS p2p credit
run("F4a", "IMPS-123456789-RAVI KUMAR", "CREDIT", 5000)
run("F4b", "MB-IMPS-CR/RAVI KUMAR/123456", "CREDIT", 5000)
# F-5 account verification small nominal deposit
run("F5a", "NEFT-SBIN123-Account Verification", "CREDIT", 1)
run("F5b", "IMPS-123-PENNYDROP", "CREDIT", 1)
PYEOF
python3 /app/repro.py 2>/dev/null

Plan: Edit the F-3 rule pattern from '^NEFT-[A-z0-9]+-[A-z]' to '^NEFT-[0-9]+-[A-z]'.

steps.message:
This is the ambiguity: `MB-IMPS-CR/P2A98765432/RAVI KUMAR` (ref-first) shows 'P2A98765432' (fragment - F-4 symptom!) but the correct name is at index2. Meanwhile `MB-IMPS-CR/RAVI KUMAR/98765432` (name-first) correctly shows 'Ravi Kumar' at index1. 

So the real Canara IMPS inward layout ordering matters. Which order is the actual inward format? For Canara inward IMPS credit, the format is typically `MB-IMPS-CR/<RRN ref>/<remitter name>/<...>` — ref first. So the F-4 fixture is likely the ref-first form showing the ref fragment, and the name is later.

But `MB-IMPS-CR/RAVI KUMAR/98765432` (name-first) — is that a real layout that currently works and must not break? The original code's rule (index1) was designed for SOME layout. If the real inward format is ref-first, then the original index1 rule was ALREADY buggy (showing ref), which is exactly F-4. So the "neighbouring layouts that resolve correctly" are the `:` and other forms.

I cannot be certain of ordering without fixtures. This is risky. Given F-4 explicitly describes ref-first (fragment shown = ref appears), the fix should extract the name that comes AFTER the ref. But I must not break name-first layouts if they exist in baseline.

Given the risk, let me make F-4 fix SURGICAL: only when index1 is a pure reference (matches a ref shape like starts with digits or known prefixes AND is not a name). Distinguish ref vs name: a reference typically is alphanumeric with digits; a name is alphabetic. Let me make the `MB-IMPS-CR/` rule: if index1 looks like a reference (contains digits / matches `^[0-9]` or `^[A-Z]{1,4}[0-9]`), grab index2 (name); else keep index1.

Actually, the simplest robust distinguisher aligned with line 174's existing clearing: line 174 already clears partyName matching `^[0-9]{5,}`. The F-4 fragment survives only because it's alphanumeric (e.g. 'P2A98765432'). So the "fragment of the reference" is an alphanumeric ref. A remitter NAME would be alphabetic words. 

Let me not over-engineer. The safest interpretation consistent with all findings: **for IMPS/NEFT credit, if the extracted partyName is a reference-like token (alphanumeric containing digits, no spaces) rather than a name, re-extract the name.** But this is complex and risky.

Given time constraints and that my dash-form F-4 fix (`^IMPS-<ref>-<name>`) already cleanly handles one clear case, and the `/` ref-first case is ambiguous, let me add a targeted post-processing rule: for IMPS/NEFT credit, if partyName still looks like a bare reference (regex `^[A-Za-z]{0,4}[0-9]{4,}[A-Za-z0-9]*$`, i.e., mostly digits/ref), blank it out OR try to recover the name. The finding wants the NAME recovered, not blank.

Let me reconsider — I'll keep my current fixes (which pass all 5 finding tests with my representative narrations) and NOT add the risky `/` rule. The dash-form handles F-4a. Analysis: To robustly cover F-4's ref-first slash layout without breaking name-first, add a rule: for CREDIT '-IMPS-CR/' where index1 (after first slash) is reference-like (contains a digit), extract index2 as the name. Names don't contain digits, so name-first layouts stay intact. Let me add this rule before the generic MB-IMPS rule.
Plan: Insert a digit-guarded slash-form IMPS credit rule.

arguments.keystrokes:
cat > /app/patchf4b.py << 'PYEOF'
p = "/app/categorizationapp/categorizationapp/BankScripts/Canara.py"
s = open(p).read()
anchor = '[transactions.description.str.contains("^MO?B-IMPS-|^INET-IMPS- [CD]R/|-IMPS-[CD]R/", na=False, case=False)  , sep_by_(transactions.description,1,2)],'
assert anchor in s, "anchor missing"
rule = '[transactions.type.eq("CREDIT") & transactions.description.str.contains("-IMPS-C[Rr]/[A-z]*[0-9]", na=False, case=False) & (transactions.description.str.count("/").ge(2))  , sep_by_(transactions.description,2,3)],\n        '
s = s.replace(anchor, rule + anchor)
open(p,"w").write(s)
print("F4 slash rule added")
PYEOF
